disclaimer: simulation is decision support, not deterministic forecast
method_note: No historical truth_set was supplied; this report provides impact analysis only, not backtest validation.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: Centre for Connected and Autonomous Vehicles
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Policy architect; strong alignment with scheme objectives, but may face pressure to balance innovation with safety.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Enforcement body; concerns about resource adequacy and clarity of enforcement powers.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: Consenting Authority (Local Licensing / Franchising Body)
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Cautious; loss of local control and automatic consent provision are key friction points.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: Traffic Authority
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Traffic management concerns; will demand impact assessments.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: Emergency Services
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Safety and access protocols needed; cautious stance.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: APS Operator (Permit Holder / Applicant)
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Supportive; sees scheme as market gateway, but concerned about compliance costs and multi-authority consent.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: Older and Disabled Passengers
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Supportive with conditions; insists on mandatory accessibility standards.

stakeholder_impact_matrix.name: General Public / Passengers
stakeholder_impact_matrix.qualitative_signal: Tech-enthusiast archetype; positive but limited direct impact until services launch.

risk_timeline.signal: Local authority pushback on automatic consent and resource burden
Legal challenges from consenting authorities over loss of control
Delays due to multi-authority consent process and 6-week response window
Safety incidents or emergency service access issues
Enforcement gaps if DVSA resources insufficient

mitigation_options.option: Extend consenting authority response window or provide resource support
mitigation_options.rationale: Reduces risk of automatic consent and local opposition.

mitigation_options.option: Mandate accessibility standards in permit conditions from day one
mitigation_options.rationale: Addresses older/disabled passenger concerns and aligns with Public Sector Equality Duty.

mitigation_options.option: Establish clear emergency vehicle override protocols before first permit
Phase fee introduction to avoid early operator burden
mitigation_options.rationale: Supports startup market entry as per assumption 10.

mitigation_options.option: Create a single consent portal for multi-authority coordination
mitigation_options.rationale: Reduces administrative complexity for operators and authorities.

confidence_notes: 无历史回测数据，仅提供影响分析。

claims_audit_table.claim: The APS permitting scheme provides a clear legal route for deploying commercial automated passenger services.
Without the APS scheme, automated services may be banned or unregulated.
Consenting authorities have 6 weeks to respond; silence gives automatic consent.
Permit holders must publish reports on steps taken for older/disabled passengers.
Civil sanctions are not implemented for pilots; variation/suspension/withdrawal is the enforcement means.
No formal court/tribunal appeals system is proposed; internal DVSA review is provided.
Fees will not be charged initially; full cost recovery fees will be introduced later.
Local consenting authorities will face resource strain from the 6-week response requirement.
Emergency services will require clear protocols for interacting with APS vehicles.
The scheme will face opposition from local authorities due to loss of control.
